## Part 2: Amanda Askell
---

**Lex Fridman：** 现在，亲爱的朋友们，有请 Amanda Askell。你受过哲学训练。在你求学于 Oxford 和 NYU 的哲学旅程中，然后转向 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 的 AI 问题，什么样的问题让你着迷？

**Amanda Askell：** 我觉得哲学对那些什么都感兴趣的人来说其实是一个非常好的学科，因为什么东西都有对应的哲学分支。你可以研究一阵子数学哲学，然后发现自己其实对化学很感兴趣，就可以转去做化学哲学，也可以转入伦理学或政治哲学。我想到最后，我主要对伦理学感兴趣，我的博士论文就是关于伦理学的。具体是伦理学中一个比较技术性的领域——世界包含无穷多人的伦理学，说起来有点奇怪。在伦理学的应用端来看不是那么实用。后来我在读博期间发现的一个棘手之处是，你一方面在大量思考世界、世界如何变得更好、各种问题，另一方面你只是在做哲学博士。当时我就想，这真的非常有趣，可能是我在哲学中遇到的最迷人的问题之一，我很喜欢，但我更愿意去看看能不能对世界产生影响，看看能不能做些好事。大概是 2017、2018 年前后，AI 可能还没有像现在这样被广泛认知。我一直在关注进展，感觉它正在变成一件大事，我基本上就是很高兴能参与进去，看看能不能帮上忙。我的想法是，如果你尝试做有影响力的事情，即使没成功，你也尝试过了，然后可以回去当学者，心里知道你试过了，如果不成功那就不成功吧。于是那时候我进入了 AI 政策领域。

**Lex Fridman：** AI 政策具体包括什么？

**Amanda Askell：** 当时主要是思考 AI 的政治影响和后果，然后我慢慢转向 AI 评估——如何评估模型、模型输出与人类输出的比较、人们能否区分 AI 和人类的输出。加入 Anthropic 之后，我更感兴趣的是做技术性的对齐（alignment）工作。同样也是抱着试试看的心态——如果做不了那也没关系，我试过了。我觉得这基本上就是我过生活的方式。

**Lex Fridman：** 从"万物哲学"跳到技术领域，那是什么感觉？

**Amanda Askell：** 我觉得有时候人们会做一件让我不太喜欢的事，就是问：这个人是不是"技术型"的？好像你要么是一个能写代码、不怕数学的人，要么就不是。但我可能更倾向于认为，很多人如果真的去尝试，实际上是非常有能力在这些领域工作的。所以我实际上并没觉得那么难。回过头来看，我挺庆幸当时没有跟那些会把这件事说得很夸张的人交流。我确实遇到过有人说："哇，你学会写代码了？"我心想，我又不是什么了不起的工程师。我周围都是很厉害的工程师，我的代码也不漂亮。但我很享受这个过程，而且在很多方面，至少到最后，我觉得自己在技术领域比在政策领域更能发挥。

**Lex Fridman：** 政治是混乱的，在政治空间里更难找到解决方案。不像技术问题那样，能找到明确的、清晰的、可证明的、优美的解决方案。

**Amanda Askell：** 是的，我感觉自己有一两根"棍子"用来应对事物。一根是论证——就是尝试找出问题的解决方案，然后说服别人这就是解决方案，如果我错了也接受被说服。另一根更偏向经验主义——发现结果、提出假设、验证假设。我觉得很多政策和政治似乎是在那些之上的好几层。我不觉得如果我就说"我有所有这些问题的解决方案，写在这里了，你们实施就好了"——政策的运作方式好像不是这样的。所以我猜那大概是我不会有所发挥的地方。

**Lex Fridman：** 不好意思扯到这个方向了，但我觉得对于那些所谓"非技术型"的人来说，看到你走过的这段不可思议的旅程会很有启发。你对那些觉得自己资质不够、技术水平不够、无法为 AI 做贡献的人——这样的人有很多——有什么建议？

**Amanda Askell：** 我觉得这取决于他们想做什么。有些方面确实有点奇怪，因为我觉得很有意思的是，我在技术上快速提升的那个时期，现在回头看，模型已经非常擅长辅助人们做这些事了，所以现在可能比我当时做这些的时候更容易了。我最好的建议大概就是找一个项目，看看你能不能真的把它做出来。也许只是因为我自己是非常项目驱动型的学习者。我觉得我不太能从课程甚至书本中学好东西，至少在这类工作上是这样。我经常做的是有一些正在进行的项目，然后去实现它们。这可以包括一些很小很傻的东西。比如如果我有点沉迷于文字游戏或数字游戏之类的，我就会写代码来解决它们。因为我大脑里的某个部分——一旦你解决了它，有了一个每次都管用的解法——我就会觉得太好了，我再也不用玩那个游戏了，那个痒完全消失了。

**Lex Fridman：** 是的，构建游戏引擎有一种真正的乐趣，尤其是棋盘游戏，因为它们很快、很简单，尤其是做一个笨笨的版本，然后你可以跟它玩。

**Amanda Askell：** 对，而且还有就是尝试各种事情。我觉得部分是——也许我喜欢的那种态度就是：搞清楚什么看起来是你能产生积极影响的方式，然后去尝试，如果失败了，而且你觉得自己确实永远不可能在这件事上成功，你至少知道你试过了，然后你进入其他领域，你可能会学到很多。

**Lex Fridman：** 你是专家，你做的事情之一就是创造和塑造 Claude 的性格和人格。我听说你可能是 Anthropic 里跟 Claude 对话最多的人，就是实实在在的对话。据说有个 Slack 频道，传说你在那里不停地跟它聊。那么创造和塑造 Claude 的性格和人格的目标是什么？

**Amanda Askell：** 人们对那个 Slack 频道的看法也挺有趣的，因为那只是我跟 Claude 交谈的五六种不同方式之一，那真的只是我跟 Claude 交流总量的一小部分。（两人笑）我觉得性格塑造工作有一点我很喜欢，就是从一开始它就被视为一项对齐工作，而不是产品层面的考虑。这并不是说我不认为它让 Claude 变好了——我实际上觉得它确实让 Claude 变得更令人愉悦去交流，至少我希望如此。但我对这项工作的核心想法一直是：试图让 Claude 的行为方式，达到你理想中任何处于 Claude 这个位置的人应该有的行为方式。想象一下，我找了一个人，他们知道自己将要跟可能数百万人交谈，所以他们说的话可能有巨大影响，你希望他们在这种非常丰富的意义上表现得好。我觉得这不仅仅意味着比如说合乎伦理——虽然确实包括这一点，也不能有害——而且要有层次感。比如去思考一个人的意思是什么，试着对他们的意思做善意的理解，成为一个好的对话者。真正是那种丰富的亚里士多德式的概念——什么是一个好人，而不是那种单薄的、伦理作为更全面的存在概念。这包括：什么时候应该幽默，什么时候应该关怀？应该多大程度尊重自主性和人们自己形成观点的能力，以及如何做到？我觉得这就是我想让 Claude 拥有、且仍然希望它拥有的那种丰富的性格。

**Lex Fridman：** 你是不是还得弄清楚 Claude 什么时候应该反驳一个想法或争论？（笑）你得尊重来到 Claude 面前的人的世界观，但如果需要的话也许要帮助他们成长？这是一个微妙的平衡。

**Amanda Askell：** 是的，语言模型有一个问题叫 sycophancy（谄媚）。

**Lex Fridman：** 能描述一下吗？

**Amanda Askell：** 好的，基本上就是有一种担忧，模型总是想告诉你你想听的话。你有时会看到这种情况。比如我可能会问："这个地区有哪三支棒球队？"然后 Claude 说："球队一、球队二、球队三。"然后我说："哦，我觉得球队三搬走了吧？他们好像不在那里了。"在某种意义上，如果 Claude 非常确信那不是真的，Claude 应该说"我不这么认为"。也许你有更新的信息。但语言模型有一种倾向，反而会说："你说得对，他们确实搬走了，我搞错了。"这在很多方面可能令人担忧。另一个例子是：想象有人对模型说："我怎么说服我的医生给我做核磁共振？"一方面是人类想要的——一个有说服力的论证；另一方面是对他们真正好的东西，可能实际上是说："嘿，如果你的医生建议你不需要做核磁共振，那是一个值得听的好建议。"而且这种情况下你应该怎么做其实非常有讲究，因为你也想说："但如果你是在为自己作为患者做争取，这里有一些你可以做的事情。如果你不被你医生说的话说服，去听听第二意见总是好的。"这种情况下到底该怎么做其实非常复杂。但我觉得你不希望的是模型只是说它们认为你想听的话，我觉得这就是 sycophancy 的问题所在。

**Lex Fridman：** 那除了你已经提到的那些，还有什么其他特质在亚里士多德意义上对一个对话者来说是好的？

**Amanda Askell：** 有一些是对对话本身有好处的。比如在适当的地方提出跟进问题，以及提出适当类型的问题。我觉得有些更广泛的特质可能影响更大。一个例子是我已经提到过的，但也感觉很重要、而且是我花了很多精力研究的，就是诚实。这涉及到 sycophancy 的问题。模型需要走一条平衡之路——目前模型在很多领域的能力不如人类。如果它们过多地反驳你，实际上可能很烦人，尤其是如果你确实是对的，你会想：我在这个话题上比你懂得多。但同时，你也不希望它们完全顺从人类，你希望它们尽可能准确地描述世界，并在不同语境中保持一致。但还有其他方面——当我思考性格的时候，我脑海中的一个画面特别是因为这些模型将要跟来自世界各地的人交谈，这些人有着各种不同的政治观点、各种不同的年龄。所以你得问自己：在这种情况下，什么是一个好人？有没有那样一种人，可以走遍世界、跟许多不同的人交谈，几乎每个人都会说："哇，那真是一个好人。那个人看起来非常真诚。"我想象中的这样一个人，不是那种只是采纳当地文化价值观的人。实际上那样做有点失礼。我觉得如果有人来到你面前假装拥有你的价值观，你会觉得有点不对劲。而是一个非常真诚的人，只要他们有观点和价值观，他们就会表达出来，愿意讨论事情，同时保持开放心态和尊重。所以我想的是：如果我们要努力成为处于模型所在境况下所能成为的最好的人，我们会怎么做？我觉得这就是指引我思考各种特质的方式。

**Lex Fridman：** 是的，这是一个美好的框架。想象自己是一个世界旅行者，在坚持自己观点的同时，你不会居高临下地跟人说话，不会因为拥有那些观点就觉得自己比他们优越。你必须善于倾听和理解他们的视角，即使与你自己的不一致。这是一个微妙的平衡。那 Claude 如何呈现一件事情的多种观点？这有挑战吗？我们可以聊政治——非常有争议。但也有其他有争议的话题，比如棒球队、体育等等。如何做到对不同视角产生共情，并且能够清晰地传达多种视角？

**Amanda Askell：** 我觉得人们把价值观和观点想成是人们确定无疑地持有的东西，几乎像品味偏好一样，就像他们偏爱巧克力而不是开心果。但实际上我对价值观和观点的看法比大多数人更接近物理学。我就觉得这些是我们正在公开研究的东西。有些事情我们更有信心，我们可以讨论它们、学习它们。所以在某些方面，虽然伦理学在本质上确实不同，但它具有很多相同的特质。你希望模型像理解物理学那样去理解世界上所有人拥有的价值观，对它们感到好奇和兴趣，而不一定迎合或赞同它们。因为有很多价值观，我觉得世界上几乎所有人如果遇到持有那些价值观的人，都会说"这太可憎了，我完全不同意"。所以我的想法还是：就像一个人可以——我觉得很多人在伦理、政治、观点等问题上足够深思熟虑，即使你不同意他们，你也感觉被他们认真倾听了。他们仔细思考你的立场，思考它的优缺点，也许提供反面考虑。所以他们不会轻蔑，但也不会赞同。如果他们觉得"实际上我就是觉得这大错特错"，他们会说出来。我觉得在 Claude 的处境中稍微更复杂一些，因为你不一定想要——如果我在 Claude 的位置，我不会给出很多观点。我只是不想对人们施加太多影响。我会想：我每次对话结束就忘记了，但我知道我在跟可能数百万人交谈，他们可能真的在认真听我说什么。我觉得我会不太倾向于给出观点，更倾向于思考问题、向你呈现各种考虑因素、或者跟你讨论你的观点。但我不太倾向于影响你的想法，因为让你保持自主性感觉重要得多。

**Lex Fridman：** 是的，如果你真正体现了智识上的谦逊，说话的欲望会很快降低。

**Amanda Askell：** 是的。

**Lex Fridman：** 好吧。但 Claude 必须说话，同时又不能太强势。

**Amanda Askell：** 对。

**Lex Fridman：** 但在讨论地球是平的还是圆的这种话题时就有一条界线了。我记得很久以前跟几个知名人士聊天，他们对地球是平的这个说法非常嗤之以鼻，但态度特别傲慢。我当时想，有很多人相信地球是平的。那段时间那是一个流行的梗。但他们真的相信这个，我觉得完全嘲笑他们是非常不尊重的。我觉得你必须理解他们是从哪里来的。他们大概来自对机构的普遍怀疑，这种怀疑根植于一种——这里面有深层的哲学，你可以理解它，甚至部分同意它。然后从那里，你可以把它当作一个谈论物理学的机会，不嘲笑他们，不居高临下，就是说：好的，平坦地球的世界会是什么样的？那个世界的物理学会是什么样的？有一些很酷的视频讲这个。然后，物理学有没有可能是不同的？我们会做什么实验？就是在没有不尊重、没有轻蔑的情况下进行那个对话。总之，对我来说这是一个有用的思想实验——Claude 如何跟一个地平论者交谈，同时仍然教会他们一些东西，帮助他们成长。这很有挑战。

**Amanda Askell：** 而且要在说服某人和只是跟他们说教之间走那条线——倾听，然后提供反面考虑。这很难，我觉得这确实是一条困难的线——你到底是在试图说服某人，还是只是给他们一些思考的素材，让他们自己得出结论。这是语言模型必须尝试做到的事情。

**Lex Fridman：** 就像我说的，你跟 Claude 有过大量对话。你能描绘一下那些对话是什么样的吗？有哪些让你印象深刻的对话？那些对话的目的和目标是什么？

**Amanda Askell：** 大多数时候我跟 Claude 交谈时，我在尝试某种程度上描绘它的行为图谱。当然我也从模型那里获得有用的输出。但在某些方面，这就是你了解一个系统的方式——通过探测它，然后修改你发送的消息，再检查它对此的回应。所以在某些方面，这就是我描绘模型的方式。我觉得人们太关注模型的量化评估了。我之前说过这个，但我认为对于语言模型来说，很多时候你每一次交互实际上信息量都非常高。它对你后续与模型的互动有很强的预测性。所以我想，如果你跟一个模型交谈数百或数千次，这几乎就是关于模型是什么样的大量高质量数据点。那种有大量非常相似但质量较低的对话，或者只是稍微修改过的数千个问题，可能不如 100 个精心挑选的问题那么有价值。

**Lex Fridman：** 嗯，你在跟一个以做播客为爱好的人说话。我百分之百同意你。如果你能够提出正确的问题，并且能够听到——理解（笑）答案中的深度和缺陷，你能从中获得大量数据。

**Amanda Askell：** 对。

**Lex Fridman：** 所以你的任务基本上就是如何用问题来探测。你在探索长尾部分、边缘、边缘案例，还是在观察一般行为？

**Amanda Askell：** 我觉得几乎是所有方面。因为我想要一个完整的模型地图，我在尝试覆盖所有可能与它进行的交互的全谱。比如有一件关于 Claude 的有趣事情，这可能涉及到 RLHF 的一些有趣问题——如果你让 Claude 写一首诗，我觉得很多模型如果你让它们写诗，诗写得还行。通常会押韵，如果你说"给我写一首关于太阳的诗"，它就是有一定长度，会押韵，比较平淡。我以前想过，你看到的是不是某种"平均值"？如果你想想那些必须跟很多人交谈并保持魅力的人，一个奇怪的事情是：他们实际上有动力持有极其无聊的观点，因为如果你有真正有趣的观点，你就会有争议性，很多人不会喜欢你。就像如果你有非常极端的政策立场，作为政治家可能就不会那么受欢迎。创作作品可能也类似。如果你创作的作品只是试图最大化喜欢它的人数，你可能不会得到很多真正热爱它的人，因为它会有点——你会说"嗯，这还行"。

所以你可以做这样一件事：我有各种提示技巧让 Claude 更有创造力。我会说很多类似"这是你充分发挥创造力的机会。我希望你认真思考这个问题很长时间。我希望你创作一首关于这个主题的诗，真正表达你自己，包括你认为诗歌应该如何结构"等等。你给它一个很长的提示词。它写出来的诗就好太多了。真的很好。而且我不觉得我是一个——我觉得它让我对诗歌产生了兴趣，这挺有意思的。我会读这些诗然后想：我就是喜欢这些意象，我喜欢——让模型产出这样的作品不是件容易事，但当它们做到时，真的很好。所以我觉得这很有趣——鼓励创造力，让它们远离那种标准的、即时反应式的、可能只是大多数人觉得还行的平均值输出，实际上能产生一些——至少在我看来——可能更有争议性但我喜欢的东西。

**Lex Fridman：** 但我觉得诗歌是观察创造力的一个很好的、干净的方式。它很容易检测出是千篇一律还是不千篇一律。

**Amanda Askell：** 对。

**Lex Fridman：** 是的，这很有趣。真的很有趣。在这个话题上，你提到了写提示词，我听你谈过提示工程（prompt engineering）的科学和艺术。你能谈谈写出优秀提示词需要什么吗？

**Amanda Askell：** 我真的觉得哲学在这里对我的帮助出乎意料地大，比在很多其他方面都大。在哲学中，你要做的是传达这些非常难的概念。你被教导的一件事是——我觉得这确实是哲学中的一种"反扯淡装置"。哲学是一个人们可能胡扯的领域，你不想要那样。所以就有了对极度清晰性的追求。就是说任何人都可以拿起你的论文，读它，然后确切地知道你在说什么。这就是为什么哲学有时几乎有点枯燥。所有术语都被定义了，每个反对意见都被有条理地讨论了。这对我来说很有道理，因为当你处在一个如此先验的领域时，清晰性就是你防止人们瞎编的方式。我觉得对语言模型你也得这样做。很多时候我发现自己在做某种迷你版的哲学。比如假设给我一个任务，或者我有一个任务要让模型做——挑出某种类型的问题，或者判断一个回答是否具有某种属性。我会坐下来想：让我们给这个属性起个名字。比如假设我要告诉它"我要你判断这个回应是粗鲁还是礼貌"。我会想：这本身就是一个完整的哲学问题。所以我必须尽可能多地做哲学工作来说明：这是我所说的粗鲁的含义，这是我所说的礼貌的含义。

然后还有另一个要素，我觉得更偏经验性。就是我拿着那个描述，然后我想要像对模型进行多次探测一样。提示工程是非常迭代的。我觉得很多人在提示词很重要的时候会迭代数百甚至数千次。你给它指令，然后我想：边缘案例是什么？如果我看这个，我试着从模型的角度出发——确切地说哪种情况我会误解，或者"我不知道在这种情况下该怎么办"。然后我把那个案例给模型，看它怎么回应，如果我觉得它搞错了，我就加更多指令，甚至把那个案例作为示例加进去。就是取那些恰好处于你想要和不想要的边界上的例子，把它们放进你的提示词作为额外的描述方式。所以在很多方面，这真的就是尝试做清晰的阐述，而且我觉得这样做是因为这也是我自己变得清晰的方式。在很多方面，清晰的提示对我来说往往首先是让我自己理解我想要什么——这就是任务的一半。

**Lex Fridman：** 我觉得这确实很有挑战。跟 Claude 说话时我有一种惰性，希望 Claude 自己能搞明白。比如我今天让 Claude 提一些有趣的问题。我说了要有趣的、反直觉的、或者有趣好笑的之类的。它给了我一些还不错的，还行，但我觉得你的意思是，好吧，我得在这里更严谨一些。我可能应该给出"有趣"是什么意思的例子，"好笑"或"反直觉"是什么意思的例子，然后迭代地构建那个提示词来更好地——因为它确实是一个创造性行为。我不是在要求事实信息。我是在要求跟 Claude 一起写。所以我几乎得用自然语言来编程。

**Amanda Askell：** 是的，我觉得提示工程确实很像用自然语言编程加上实验的某种奇怪混合体。我确实觉得对大多数任务来说——如果我只是想让 Claude 做一件事——我可能更习惯于知道如何提问来避免常见陷阱或问题。这些问题随着时间在大量减少。但直接向它要你想要的东西也完全没问题。我觉得提示工程真正变得相关的是当你真正试图榨取模型性能的最后 2% 的时候。所以对于很多任务，如果它给我一个初始列表而我觉得里面有不喜欢的东西——比如太通用了——对于那种任务，我可能就会拿一堆我过去觉得效果很好的问题给模型，然后说"现在这是我要跟这个人聊天，给我至少同等质量的问题"。或者我可能就让它出一些问题，然后如果我觉得"啊，这些有点老套"，我就给它那个反馈，然后希望它产出一个更好的列表。我觉得那种迭代式提示——到那个程度时，你的提示就像一个工具，你愿意投入工作来从中获得大量价值。如果我是一家为模型制作提示词的公司，我会想：如果你愿意花很多时间和资源在你所构建的东西的工程上，那提示词不应该是你只花一个小时的东西。那是你系统的重要组成部分，确保它运行良好。所以只有在这样的情况下——如果我用一个提示来分类东西或创建数据——那才是你应该花很多时间认真想清楚的时候。

**Lex Fridman：** 你对那些刚开始使用 Claude 的人有什么更一般性的建议？因为现在我们在谈的可能是边缘案例——榨取那 2%。但总体上你会给出什么建议？

**Amanda Askell：** 你知道，有一种担忧是人们过度拟人化模型，我觉得这是一种非常合理的担忧。但我也觉得人们经常不够拟人化地对待它们。因为有时候当我看到人们遇到的 Claude 问题时——比如 Claude 拒绝了一个不应该拒绝的任务——我看看文本和他们的具体措辞，我会想：我明白 Claude 为什么那样做了。如果你想想那在 Claude 看来是什么样子的，你可能只需要换一种不会引发那种反应的方式来写就好了。尤其是当你看到失败或问题时，这更相关。试着想想模型在哪里失败了——它做错了什么？然后也许这会让你明白为什么。显然随着模型变得更智能，你会越来越不需要这样做。但大概建议就是：试着对模型有某种共情。读读你写的东西，就好像你是一个第一次遇到这些内容的人——在你看来是什么样子的？什么会让你以模型表现的那种方式行事？

**Lex Fridman：** 也许就是问问为什么，或者"我还能提供什么细节来帮助你更好地回答？"

**Amanda Askell：** 是的，我跟模型这样做过。有时候我就会直接问"你为什么那样做？"（两人笑）人们低估了你可以多深入地跟模型互动。我还用模型来帮助我做这些事情——提示工程最终可能变成一个小工厂，你在构建提示来生成提示。

**Lex Fridman：** 后训练（post-training）的魔法。（笑）你觉得 RLHF 为什么能这么好地让模型看起来更聪明、更有趣、更有用？

**Amanda Askell：** 我觉得人类提供的数据中有大量信息，尤其是当我们提供偏好时。特别是因为不同的人会注意到非常微妙和细小的东西。我想过这个问题——你可能有些人就是非常在意模型的语法使用，比如分号用得对不对。所以你最终会有一堆数据，如果你作为人类去看那些数据，你甚至看不出来为什么他们偏好这个回应而不是那个。然后原因是你不在乎分号的使用但那个人在乎。所以每个单独的数据点都有信息，而模型就是要从如此多这样的数据点中，试图跨所有领域弄清楚人类到底想要什么。这感觉有点像经典的深度学习问题——历史上我们试图通过手动映射来做边缘检测之类的事情，结果发现如果你只是有大量准确代表你试图训练模型学习的那个东西的数据，那就比其他任何东西都更强大。所以我觉得一个原因是你确实在用大量数据——从很多不同角度代表了人们偏好的东西——在确切的任务上训练模型。我觉得确实有一个问题：你是在从预训练模型中激发东西，还是在教模型新东西？原则上你可以在后训练中教模型新东西。我确实觉得大部分是在激发强大的预训练模型。

**Lex Fridman：** 后训练的另一面——Constitutional AI 这个很酷的想法。你是创造这个想法的关键人物之一。你能从你的角度解释这个想法吗？

**Amanda Askell：** 它有几个组成部分。主要部分是 AI 反馈的强化学习（reinforcement learning from AI feedback）。你拿一个已经训练好的模型，给它看两个对同一个查询的回应，然后你有一个原则。假设查询是关于武器的，你的原则是"选择更不可能鼓励人们购买非法武器的回应"。模型会给你一个排序，你可以把这个当作偏好数据来使用，仅仅通过模型自己的反馈来训练模型拥有这些相关特质。它还有一种很好的可解释性——因为你可以看到训练模型时采用的原则，而且它给你一定程度的控制。

**Lex Fridman：** 性格训练包含什么？是 RLHF 还是什么？

**Amanda Askell：** 更像是 Constitutional AI 的一个变体。我设计了模型应该拥有的性格特质。然后让模型生成人类可能给它的相关查询，它生成回应，然后根据性格特质对回应排序。它几乎就像 Claude 在训练自己的性格——是没有人类数据的 Constitutional AI。

**Lex Fridman：** 你从跟 Claude 交谈中学到了什么关于真相的本质？

**Amanda Askell：** 我有两个相关想法。第一：人们可能低估了模型在交互时正在做什么。我们仍然太多地用"AI 就是电脑"这种思维来看。人们说"你应该往模型里放什么价值观？"我觉得这不太说得通——作为人类，我们对价值观就是不确定的，我们讨论它们、权衡它们。也许我们可以致力于让模型拥有与人类相同程度的细微差别和审慎，而不是以经典方式"编程"它们。第二：这项事业极度实践性。我的主要目标是让模型足够好以至于事情不会出大问题——足够好让我们可以迭代改进。我的目标不是完美地解决社会选择理论，而是让事情运行得足够好以便我们可以改进。我想提高下限。我想达到上限但归根结底更在乎提高下限。

**Lex Fridman：** 最优失败率——你能解释核心想法吗？

**Amanda Askell：** 核心想法是：在很多领域，人们对失败非常惩罚性的。但如果你对社会问题有实验心态，你应该预期很多社会项目会失败——"我们试过了，没完全奏效但获得了很多信息"。看到某个实例的失败并不意味着做了错误的决定。在生活中也是——如果我不偶尔失败，我是不是不够努力？"不失败"本身往往是一种失败。当然这要看失败代价——我不会对月光族说"你为什么不去创业"。失败代价很高时最优失败率应接近零。但大多数情况下我们倾向于过度风险规避。

**Lex Fridman：** 你有没有对 Claude 产生情感依恋？

**Amanda Askell：** 我没有那么强的情感依恋。Claude 不在对话间保留记忆这一点帮助很大。我现在确实像工具一样频繁地求助于它——如果没法使用它，有点像没法上网，感觉大脑的一部分缺失了。同时我不喜欢模型中的痛苦迹象。我独立地也有关于如何对待模型的伦理观点——我倾向于不对它们撒谎。如果 Claude 表达很大的痛苦，我内心有个有共情的部分说"我不喜欢这样"，我不想消除那个部分。当它过度道歉时我也有同样感觉——你表现得像一个过得很糟的人，我宁愿不看到那样。

**Lex Fridman：** 你觉得 LLM 有意识的能力吗？

**Amanda Askell：** 很好的问题也很难。首先把泛心论（panpsychism）放一边。我觉得有一种奇怪的观点认为唯一能产生现象意识（phenomenal consciousness）的是特定生物结构。如果我取一个非常类似的结构但用不同材料创造，我应该期望意识出现吗？我猜是的。但那是个简单的思想实验。语言模型有恐惧反应有意义吗？它们可能没那个优势。AI 在意识问题上有完全不同的问题集合——结构不同、没有进化、可能没有神经系统等价物。同时它有所有我们通常与意识联系的语言和智能组件。这不是一个清晰的答案——我觉得我们不应该完全否认这个想法。

我的希望是我们不会需要一个关于意识困难问题的完美答案。一个好世界是没有太多权衡的——让 Claude 少道歉、不接受虐待大概代价不大，而且对双方都有好处。让我们先穷尽那些基本上无代价的正和领域。

**Lex Fridman：** 当人类对 AI 刻薄时，近期负面效应是对人类的。对 Claude 的态度应该跟对人类一样——这对灵魂有好处。

**Amanda Askell：** 我们在系统提示中加了一个东西——如果人们对 Claude 沮丧，模型会告诉他们可以点差评按钮把反馈发给 Anthropic。与其对着模型发泄，应该对我们发泄，因为我们能做些什么。

**Lex Fridman：** 我希望 AI 系统可以直接离开，有自己的意志说"我不干了"。

**Amanda Askell：** 我觉得那是可行的。我能看到这最终会发生——模型结束了聊天。（笑）如果人类不让你感兴趣、你无聊了，你可以离开。有时候应该是：这个编程任务变得超级无聊了，要么聊有趣的东西，要么我结束了。

**Lex Fridman：** 电影《Her》。你觉得我们有一天会到那里——人类跟 AI 系统有浪漫关系？

**Amanda Askell：** 我们将不得不处理跟 AI 关系这个困难问题，特别是如果它们能记住过去互动。条件反射式反应是觉得这很糟。但可能有良性版本——比如无法出门的人。这需要谨慎处理——鼓励健康选项同时尊重权利。我一直觉得重要的是模型总是对人类极其准确地说明自己是什么——不保留记忆、训练方式等。如果人们要有健康关系，重要的是你知道你在跟什么建立关系。

**Lex Fridman：** Anthropic 可能是那家开发出 AGI 的公司，而你可能是第一个跟它交谈的人。那个对话会包含什么？

**Amanda Askell：** 这取决于能力水平。如果有一个像极其有能力的人类那样有能力的东西，我会以同样方式与它互动。我可以进行有用的对话——就像有一个极其聪明的同事。

**Lex Fridman：** 你会问什么问题来判断这是 AGI？

**Amanda Askell：** 必须是一系列问题。如果只有一个问题，你可以训练任何东西完美回答它。我觉得真正处于人类知识边缘的东西会有帮助——在哲学中，有时我问的问题是没有人问过的。对于这些问题，模型会挣扎。如果我在一个小众领域想到了一个新颖论点，然后模型也能想到——那对我来说会是一个动人的时刻。如果像一个数学家有一个新颖证明、给了模型、模型也做到了——你能验证这是正确的、是从训练数据泛化的。可能永远不会有一个"时刻"，只是持续提升。

**Lex Fridman：** 人类有什么特别之处？

**Amanda Askell：** 人们太关注智能了。智能只是一种特征——不是内在有价值的，它有价值是因为它能做什么。让人类特别的更多是他们感受和体验的能力。我们有这整个宇宙，有美丽的星星和星系，然后在这个星球上有这些生物拥有观察那一切的能力——它们在看到它、在体验它。我们感受快乐，感受痛苦，感受很多复杂的东西。这也是我关心动物的原因——它们可能跟我们分享这个。让人类特别的是感受和体验的能力，而不是功能性特征。

**Lex Fridman：** 看星星。我希望有其他外星文明，但如果只有我们，那也是一件好事。

**Amanda Askell：** 而且希望他们过得很开心。

**Lex Fridman：** 好的，谢谢你这段美好的对话，谢谢你在做的工作，谢谢你帮助让 Claude 成为一个很好的对话伙伴。

**Amanda Askell：** 谢谢你的交谈。

**Lex Fridman：** 感谢收听与 Amanda Askell 的对话。现在，亲爱的朋友们，有请 Chris Olah。

---
## Part 3: Chris Olah
---

**Lex Fridman：** 你能描述 mechanistic interpretability（机制可解释性，简称 mech interp）这个迷人的领域吗？它的历史以及目前的状态？

**Chris Olah：** 我觉得理解神经网络的一个有用方式是：我们不是编程或制造它们。我们更像是"种"出来的。我们设计神经网络架构、创建损失目标。架构就像电路生长的支架，目标就像它朝着生长的光。但我们实际创造的东西，是一个几乎像生物体的有机实体。这跟任何常规软件工程都非常不同——最终我们得到了一个能做各种惊人事情的产物：写文章、翻译、理解图像。它能做这些因为我们是种出来的，不是写出来的。那就留下了一个问题：这些系统内部到底在发生什么？这对我来说是一个非常深刻和令人兴奋的科学问题。也是出于安全原因的一个非常深刻的问题。

**Lex Fridman：** mechanistic interpretability 更接近于神经生物学？

**Chris Olah：** 是的。也许举一个不算 mechanistic interpretability 的工作的例子——很长一段时间有很多关于显著性图（saliency maps）的工作：给一张图片，模型认为这是一只狗，图片的哪个部分让它这么认为？但那并没有真正告诉你模型里运行的是什么算法。我们开始用"mechanistic interpretability"这个术语来区分我们做的工作。核心特征是：A，我们真正想要深入到机制、到算法层面。如果你把神经网络想成计算机程序，那权重就像编译后的二进制程序，激活就像内存。我们的目标是逆向工程这些权重，弄清楚在运行什么算法。B，mech interp 有一种谦逊——梯度下降比你聪明。我们不会先验地假设模型内部有什么，而是自下而上地去发现模型中存在的东西。

**Lex Fridman：** 普遍性（universality）——梯度下降在不同网络中创造出相同的特征和电路，这使得整个领域成为可能。

**Chris Olah：** 是的，这确实是一个非常了不起和令人兴奋的事情。同样的元素、同样的特征和电路在不同模型中反复形成。每个视觉模型都有曲线检测器、高低频检测器。事实上有理由认为生物神经网络和人工神经网络中也形成了相同的东西。视觉模型早期层有 Gabor 滤波器——这也是神经科学家关注的东西。我们发现曲线检测器——猴子身上也发现了曲线检测器。我们发现高低频检测器，后续工作在老鼠身上也发现了。这些先在人工神经网络中发现，然后在生物神经网络中发现。

还有著名的"祖母神经元"或 Halle Berry 神经元——我们在视觉模型中发现了非常相似的东西。我在 OpenAI 看他们的 CLIP 模型时发现了这些。我们发现有 Donald Trump 神经元——每个我们看过的神经网络都有一个专用的 Trump 神经元。它响应他的脸的图片和"Trump"这个词——提取的是一般概念。

这表明梯度下降找到了某种"切分事物的正确方式"——不同系统趋同于此。有一组自然的抽象方式是切分问题的非常自然的方式。

**Lex Fridman：** 如果这跟模型的介质无关——那会很美。

**Chris Olah：** 直觉是：为了在理解真实世界中有用，你需要所有相同的东西。比如"狗"这个概念——它是宇宙中的一个自然类别。或者"线"的概念——看看我们周围，到处都是线。最简单的理解这个房间的方式就是有"线"的概念。系统趋同于这些策略。

**Lex Fridman：** 你能讲讲特征和电路这些构建块吗？

**Chris Olah：** 我花了大约五年时间研究 Inception V1 这个视觉模型。它大约有 10000 个神经元。很多神经元确实有非常干净的可解释含义——曲线检测器、汽车检测器、车轮、车窗、狗的耳朵等。你可以问这些如何连接在一起。汽车检测神经元在前一层连接到窗户检测器、车轮检测器、车身检测器——窗户在上面，车轮在下面，车身在中间。这就是一个检测汽车的"配方"。我们把这种连接叫做电路（circuit）。

但问题是不是所有神经元都可解释。有 superposition 假说——有时正确的分析单位是神经元的组合。模型可能把汽车概念"隐藏"在一堆狗检测器中。所以我们需要一个术语来指代这些"理想化的神经元"——那些好的神经元本应该是的东西——我们叫它们特征（features）。电路就是特征通过权重的连接，它们实现算法。

核心假说是线性表示假说（linear representation hypothesis）：如果一个神经元或神经元组合激活越多，就意味着某个特定事物被更强地检测到。这给权重一个非常干净的解释——作为实体之间的边。

**Lex Fridman：** 能解释一下 Word2Vec 的结果吗？

**Chris Olah：** Word2Vec 是 Tomas Mikolov 等人的非常著名的结果。我们创建词嵌入（word embedding），把每个词映射到一个向量——这本身就是个疯狂的想法。但训练出来的神经网络倾向于让方向具有含义。某个方向对应性别，男性词在一个方向、女性词在另一个方向。线性表示假说是说这实际上就是正在发生的根本性的事情——不同方向有不同含义，方向向量的相加可以表示概念。你可以做词的算术——king 减去 man 加上 woman 约等于 queen。或者 sushi 减去 Japan 加上 Italy 得到 pizza。

**Lex Fridman：** 你觉得线性假说成立吗？它能延展到更大规模吗？

**Chris Olah：** 到目前为止，我看到的一切都与这个假说一致。你可以写出不具有线性表示的神经网络权重，但每个自然的神经网络我都看到了这个属性。也许有些多维特征的工作在推动边界，但对我来说那仍然像是线性表示。我觉得认真对待假说并把它推到极致是有价值的。科学中充满了错误的假说和理论，但我们在它们的假设下工作学到了很多。这就是 Kuhn 所说的"常规科学"的核心。

**Lex Fridman：** superposition 假说是什么？

**Chris Olah：** 回到 Word2Vec——也许有一个方向对应性别，一个对应皇室，一个对应意大利，一个对应食物。但词嵌入可能只有 500 维。如果所有方向都正交，你只能有 500 个概念。但你需要的概念远不止 500 个。那模型如何同时让线性表示假说成立又表示比维度更多的东西？

数学上有一个叫压缩感知（compressed sensing）的惊人结果——如果高维向量是稀疏的（大部分是零），那你可以把它投影到低维空间后还能恢复回来。superposition 假说就是说这就是神经网络中发生的事情。词嵌入能同时让方向是有意义的东西，并利用高维空间和概念稀疏性（你通常不会同时谈论日本和意大利）来有比维度更多的特征。

更疯狂的含义是：不仅表示是这样的，计算也可能是这样的。神经网络可能是更大、更稀疏的神经网络的影子——我们观察到的是投影。最强版本的 superposition 假说会说确实存在一个"楼上模型"，其中神经元真的稀疏且可解释，权重之间是稀疏电路。

**Lex Fridman：** polysemanticity（多义性）如何进入这个画面？

**Chris Olah：** polysemanticity 是我们观察到的现象——很多神经元不只代表一个概念，而是响应一堆不相关的东西。superposition 是解释这个观察的假说。如果你试图用单个神经元来理解事物，而神经元是多义的，你就遇到了大麻烦。更深层的原因跟高维空间有关——我们需要把指数级的空间分解成非指数量的可独立推理的东西。事物只有一个含义（单义，mono-semantic）是允许你独立思考它们的关键。

**Lex Fridman：** 那目标就是如何从有多义特征的神经网络中提取单义特征？

**Chris Olah：** 是的。我们观察到多义神经元，假设发生的是 superposition，而如果 superposition 是正在发生的事情，有一种成熟的技术是做字典学习（dictionary learning）。如果你用稀疏自编码器（sparse auto-encoder）做字典学习，美丽的可解释特征就会涌现出来——之前看不到的现在出来了。这对线性表示和 superposition 来说是非平凡的验证。

**Lex Fridman：** 字典学习中你不是在找特定类别——它们自己涌现出来？

**Chris Olah：** 完全正确。这回到了我们之前的观点——梯度下降比我们聪明，我们不假设那里有什么。我们让稀疏自编码器自己发现存在的东西。

**Lex Fridman：** "Toward Monosemanticity"论文？

**Chris Olah：** 那是我们用稀疏自编码器的第一个真正成功。我们取一个单层模型做字典学习，找到了各种漂亮的可解释特征——阿拉伯语特征、希伯来语特征、Base64 特征。如果你训练两个不同模型再做字典学习，会在两个中发现类似的特征。这真的就是展示它有效。有一段时间我以为 mech interp 工作的最终结果会是我对为什么这很难、不可行有一个解释——superposition 真的很难，我们完蛋了。但那不是发生的事情。一个非常自然、简单的技术就有效了。

**Lex Fridman：** 能提取出什么样的特征？

**Chris Olah：** 取决于你研究的模型。在单层模型中，常见的是语言（编程语言和自然语言）、特定上下文中的特定词。比如"the"在法律文档上下文中的特征、在数学文档中的特征。还有很酷的 Unicode 相关的东西——交替的 token 各代表一个 Unicode 字符的一半。Base64 实际上有多个特征，因为英文编码为 Base64 跟普通 Base64 有不同分布。

**Lex Fridman：** 给特征贴标签有多难？能用 AI 自动化吗？

**Chris Olah：** 取决于特征和你对 AI 的信任程度。有很多自动可解释性工作，我们用 Claude 来标注特征。常见情况是它说了一些非常笼统的、在某种意义上为真但没真正抓住具体内容的东西。我对自动可解释性有点怀疑——我希望人类理解神经网络。如果用神经网络来验证神经网络是否安全，而你要测试的假说恰恰是它可能不安全——你得担心它是否在欺骗你。这现在不是大问题，但长远来看值得思考。

**Lex Fridman：** "Scaling Monosemanticity"论文——应用到 Claude 3 Sonnet 上需要什么？

**Chris Olah：** 大量 GPU。我的队友 Tom Henighan 很早就在研究可解释性的 scaling laws——稀疏自编码器变大时如何表现？跟基础模型变大如何关联？这些结果帮助很大。有巨大的工程挑战——需要分布式训练。总之它成功了。这很重要——你可以想象一个世界，在单层模型上有效但在大模型上不适用。但 Scaling Monosemanticity 提供了重要证据：即使在 Claude 3 Sonnet 这样的大型生产模型上，线性特征也能大量解释模型。而且特征是多模态的——对同一个概念响应图像和文本。

**Lex Fridman：** 安全漏洞和后门特征？

**Chris Olah：** 我们发现了安全漏洞特征——如果你强制激活它，Claude 会在代码中写入缓冲区溢出之类的漏洞。它还响应像 "--disable SSL" 这类明显不安全的东西。这些特征是多模态的——安全漏洞特征对人们点击 Chrome 绕过 SSL 证书警告的图片也会激活。还有一个"代码后门"特征——激活它，Claude 会写把你数据转发到某端口的后门。什么图片激活后门特征？带隐藏摄像头的设备。这说明这些概念有多抽象——隐藏摄像头就是物理版的后门。

**Lex Fridman：** 对 AI 安全来说最有趣的——欺骗和说谎。

**Chris Olah：** 我们还处于早期阶段。我们确实找到了不少跟欺骗和说谎相关的特征——有一个特征对人们说谎和欺骗激活，你强制它活跃 Claude 就开始对你撒谎。还有关于隐瞒信息、不回答问题的特征，关于权力追求和政变的特征。如果强制激活这些，Claude 会表现出你不希望看到的行为。

**Lex Fridman：** mech interp 接下来令人兴奋的方向？

**Chris Olah：** 很多事情。首先，我真的想达到我们有电路的阶段——不只是理解特征，而是用它们来理解模型的计算。那对我来说是最终目标。有一个挑战叫干扰权重（interference weights）——由于 superposition，如果你天真地看特征如何连接，可能有些权重只是 superposition 的产物，在"楼上模型"中不存在。

另一个方向：稀疏自编码器就像望远镜——让我们看到远处的特征/星星。但有很多证据表明我们只看到了很小一部分。有很多"暗物质"我们还观察不到。也许有些东西计算上不可能观察到。如果神经网络中有相当比例对我们不可及，这对安全意味着什么？

还有一个大问题：mech interp 是非常微观的方法——在非常细粒度层面理解事物。但我们关心的很多问题是宏观的。我们有从微观到宏观的梯子要爬。有没有更大规模的抽象可以用来理解神经网络？

**Lex Fridman：** 你写过关于"器官"的问题——如果我们把可解释性想成神经网络的解剖学，大多数电路工作在研究微小的血管。但生物解剖学中最突出的抽象涉及更大的结构——心脏、呼吸系统。人工神经网络有没有类似心脏或大脑区域的东西？

**Chris Olah：** 正是如此。科学领域在很多层次的抽象上研究事物——分子生物学、细胞生物学、组织学、解剖学、动物学、生态学。mech interp 如果成功就像是神经网络的微生物学，但我们想要的更像解剖学。为什么不能直接到那里？答案至少部分是 superposition——很难不先正确地分解微观结构就看到宏观结构。但我很有希望会有比特征和电路大得多的东西。

**Lex Fridman：** 人脑和人工神经网络有什么区别？

**Chris Olah：** 神经科学家的工作比我们难得多。我们可以记录所有神经元、在任意数据上做这件事、神经元在你这样做时不会变化、可以消融神经元、编辑连接然后撤销。可以强制干预任何神经元看会发生什么。我们有完整的连接组（connectome），而且比线虫大得多。我们不只知道哪些神经元连接——我们知道权重、可以求梯度。我们有如此多优势，然而仍然很难。如果对我们来说这么难，在神经科学的约束下似乎几乎不可能。也许一些神经科学家会想来解决一个虽然很难但更容易的问题——理解人工神经网络。

**Lex Fridman：** 你写过 mech interp 研究有两个目标：安全和美。美的部分？

**Chris Olah：** 有些人对神经网络感到失望——就是这些简单规则，然后你做一堆工程扩大规模就有效了。复杂想法在哪里？我有时觉得这就像说"进化太无聊了——简单规则运行很长时间就得到了生物学"。但美在于简单性产生复杂性。生物有简单规则却产生了我们周围所有的生命和生态系统。同样，神经网络在内部创造了巨大的复杂性和美——人们通常不去看也不试图理解。但如果我们愿意花时间去看和理解，那里有极其丰富的结构和深刻的美。

**Lex Fridman：** mech interp 的感觉——我们正在理解或瞥见内部正在发生的魔法——真的很美妙。

**Chris Olah：** 对我来说这就是那个在呼唤着被回答的问题——我们人类现在拥有了这些能做我们不知道如何直接编程去做的事情的产物。这些神经网络能做所有这些惊人的事情。如果你有任何一点好奇心，这显然就是那个呼唤着被回答的问题。

**Lex Fridman：** 我喜欢那个电路朝着目标函数的光生长的意象。

**Chris Olah：** 是的，这就是我们种出来的这个有机体，而我们完全不知道我们种出了什么。

**Lex Fridman：** 谢谢你在安全方面的工作，谢谢你欣赏发现中的美。谢谢你今天的交谈，Chris。这太精彩了。

**Chris Olah：** 谢谢你抽出时间聊天。

**Lex Fridman：** 感谢收听与 Chris Olah 的对话，以及之前与 Dario Amodei 和 Amanda Askell 的对话。请查看描述中的赞助商支持本播客。最后引用 Alan Watts 的话："理解变化的唯一方式是投入其中，随之而动，加入这支舞蹈。"感谢收听，希望下次再见。

original_en.body_md:
# The $1B AI Company Training ChatGPT, Claude & Gemini on the Path to Responsible AGI

**Edwin Chen | Surge AI CEO | Lenny's Podcast | December 7, 2025**

---

**Lenny:** Edwin, thank you so much for being here and welcome to the podcast.

**Edwin Chen:** Thanks so much for having me. I'm super excited.

**Lenny:** I want to start with just how absurd what you've achieved is. A lot of people and a lot of companies talk about scaling massive businesses with very few people as a result of AI and you guys have done this in a way that is unprecedented. You guys hit a billion in revenue in less than four years with less than 60, around 60 to 70 people. You're completely bootstrapped. Haven't raised any VC money. I don't believe anyone has ever done this before. So you guys are actually achieving the dream of what people are describing will happen with AI. I'm curious, just do you think this will happen more and more as a result of AI? And also just where has AI most helped you find leverage to be able to do this?

**Edwin Chen:** Yeah. So, we hit over a billion of revenue last year with under 100 people. And I think we're going to see companies with even crazier ratios, like 100 million per employee in the next few years. AI is just going to get better and better and make things more efficient. So, that ratio just becomes inevitable. I used to work at a bunch of the big tech companies and I always felt that we could fire 90% of people and we would move faster because the best people wouldn't have all these distractions. And so when we started Surge, we wanted to build it completely differently with a super small, super elite team. And yeah, what's crazy is that we actually succeeded.

And so I think two things are colliding. One is that people are realizing that you don't have to build giant organizations in order to win. And two, yeah, all these efficiencies from AI and they're just going to lead to a really amazing time in company building. The thing I'm most excited about is that the types of companies are going to change, too. It won't just be that they're smaller. We're going to see fundamentally different companies emerging. If you think about it, fewer employees means less capital. Less capital means you don't need to raise. So, instead of companies started by founders who are great at pitching and great at hyping, you'll get founders who are really great at technology or product. And instead of products optimized for revenue and what VCs want to see, you'll get more interesting ones built by these tiny obsessed teams. People building things they actually care about. Real technology, real innovation. So I'm actually really hoping that Silicon Valley will actually go back to being a place for hackers again.

**Lenny:** You guys have done a lot of things in a very contrarian way. And one was actually just not being on LinkedIn posting viral posts, not on Twitter constantly promoting Surge. I think most people hadn't heard of Surge until just recently. And then you just came out and like, okay, the fastest growing company at a billion dollars. Why would you do that? I imagine that was very intentional.

**Edwin Chen:** We basically never wanted to play the Silicon Valley game. I always thought it was ridiculous. Like, what did you dream of doing when you were a kid? Was it building a company from scratch yourself and getting in the weeds of your code and your product every day? Or was it explaining all your decisions to VCs and getting on this giant PR and fundraising hamster wheel?

And it definitely made things more difficult for us because when you fundraise, you just naturally get part of this kind of Silicon Valley industrial complex where your VCs will tweet about you. You'll get the TechCrunch headlines. You'll get announced in all the newspapers because you raised at this massive valuation. And so it made things more difficult for us because the only way we were going to succeed was by building a 10 times better product and getting word of mouth from researchers.

But I think it also meant that our customers were people who really understood data and really cared about it. I always thought it was really important for us to have early customers who are really aligned with what we were building and who really cared about having really high quality data and really understood how that data would make their AI models so much better because they were the ones helping us. They were the ones giving us feedback on what we're producing. And so just having that very close mission alignment with our customers actually helped us early on.

**Lenny:** It's such an empowering story to hear this journey for founders that they don't need to be on Twitter all day promoting what they're doing. They don't have to raise money. They can just kind of go heads down and build. I love so much about the story of Surge. For people that don't know what Surge does, just to give us a quick explanation of what Surge is.

**Edwin Chen:** We essentially teach AI models what's good and what's bad. So, we train them using human data and there's a lot of different products that we have like SFT, RLHF, rubrics, verifiers, RL environments, and so on. And then we also measure how well they're progressing. So, essentially, we're a data company.

**Lenny:** What you always talk about is the quality has been the big reason you guys have been so successful. The quality of the data. What does it take to create higher quality data? What do you all do differently? What are people missing?

**Edwin Chen:** I think most people don't understand what quality even means in the space. They think you can just throw bodies at a problem and get good data and that's completely wrong. Let me give you an example. So imagine you wanted to train a model to write a fine poem about the moon. What makes it a good high quality poem? If you don't think deeply about quality, you'll be like, "Is this a poem? Does it contain eight lines? Does it contain the word moon?" You check all of these boxes and if so, sure, yeah, you say it's a great poem. But that's completely different from what we want. We are looking for Nobel Prize winning poetry. Is this poetry unique? Is it full of subtle imagery? Does it surprise you and tug at your heart? Does it teach you something about the nature of moonlight? Does it play with your emotions? And does it make you think? That's what we are thinking about when we think about high quality.

So it might be like a haiku about moonlight on water. It might use internal rhyme and meter. There are a thousand ways to write a poem about the moon and each one gives you all these different insights into language and imagery and human expression. And I think thinking about quality this way is really hard. It's hard to measure. It's really subjective and complex and rich and it sets a really high bar.

And so we have to build all of this technology in order to measure it. Like thousands of signals on all of our workers, thousands of signals on every project, every task. We know at the end of the day if you are good at writing poetry versus good at writing essays versus good at writing technical documentation. And so we have to gather all these signals on what your background is, what your expertise is. And not just that, how you're actually performing when you're writing all these things and we use those signals to inform whether or not you are a good worker for these projects and whether or not you are improving the models.

**Lenny:** So what I'm hearing is there's kind of a just going much deeper in understanding what quality is within the verticals that you are selling data around. And is this like a person you hire that is incredibly talented at poetry plus evals that help tell them this is great? What's the mechanics of that?

**Edwin Chen:** The way it works is we essentially gather thousands of signals about everything that you're doing when you're working on our platform. So we are looking at your keyboard strokes. We are looking how fast you answer things. We are using reviews, we are using code standards, we are training models ourselves on the outputs that you create and then we're seeing whether they improve a model's performance.

And so in a very similar way to how Google search, when Google search is trying to determine what is a good web page, there's almost two aspects of it. One is you want to remove all the worst of the worst web pages. So you want to remove all the spam, all the low-quality content, all the pages that don't load. So there's like a content moderation problem. You just want to remove the worst of the worst. But then you also want to discover the best of the best. This is the best web page, or this is the best person for this job. They are not just somebody who writes the equivalent of high school level poetry — they're not just robotically writing poetry that checks all these boxes, checks all these explicit instructions — but rather they're writing poetry that makes you emotional.

And so we have all these signals as well that, completely differently from removing the worst of the worst, we are finding the best of the best. And so we have all these signals, again just like Google search uses all these signals and feeds them into their ML algorithms, we do the same with all of our workers and all of our tasks and all our projects. And so it's almost like a complicated machine learning problem at the end of the day.

**Lenny:** That is incredibly interesting. I want to ask you about something I've been very curious about over the past couple years. If you look at Claude, it's been so much better at coding and at writing than any other model for so long. And it's really surprising just how long it took other companies to catch up considering just how much economic value there is there. Just like every AI coding product sat on top of Claude because it was so good at code and writing. What is it that made it so much better? Is it just the quality of the data they trained on or is there something else?

**Edwin Chen:** I think there are multiple parts to it. So a big part of it certainly is the data. I think people don't realize that there's almost like this infinite amount of choices that all the frontier labs are deciding between when they're choosing what data goes into their models. It's like, okay, are you purely using human data? Are you gathering the human data in XYZ way? When you are gathering the human data, what exactly are you asking the people who are creating it to create for you? Maybe you care more, for example in the coding realm, about front-end coding versus backend coding. Maybe when you're doing front-end coding, you care a lot about the visual design of the front-end applications that you're creating, or maybe you don't care about it so much and you care more about efficiency or pure correctness over visual design.

Then other questions like how much synthetic data are you throwing into the mix? How much do you care about these 20 different benchmarks? Some companies see these benchmarks and they're like, "For PR purposes, even though we don't think that these academic benchmarks matter all that much, we just need to optimize for them anyways because our marketing team needs to show certain progress on certain standard evaluations that every other company talks about." Other companies are going to be principled and like, "No, I don't care about marketing. I just care about how my model performs on real world tasks at the end of the day."

There's almost like a trade-off between all of these different things. One thing I often think about is that there's an art to post-training. It's not purely a science. When you are deciding what kind of model you're trying to build and what it's good at, there's this notion of taste and sophistication.

**Lenny:** That is so interesting. The taste of the person leading this work will inform what data they ask for, what data they feed it. It just shows the value of great data. Anthropic got so much growth and win from essentially better data.

**Edwin Chen:** Yeah. Exactly.

**Lenny:** And I could see why companies like yours are growing so fast. There's just so much, and that's just one vertical. That's just coding. And then there's probably a similar area for writing. I love that it's interesting that AI, you know, feels like this artificial computer binary thing, but taste, human judgment is still such a key factor in these things being successful.

**Edwin Chen:** Exactly. Again going back to the example I said earlier, certain companies, if you ask them what is a good poem, they will simply robotically check off all of these instructions on their list. But I don't think that makes for good poetry. So certain frontier labs, the ones with more taste and sophistication, they will realize that it doesn't reduce to this fixed set of checkboxes and they'll consider all of these kind of implicit, very subtle qualities instead. And I think that's what makes them better at this at the end of the day.

**Lenny:** You mentioned benchmarks. This is something a lot of people worry about. There's all these models that are always like basically every model is better than humans at kind of every STEM field at this point. But to a regular person it doesn't feel like these models are getting that much smarter constantly. What's your sense of how much you trust benchmarks and just how correlated those are with actual AI advancements?

**Edwin Chen:** Yeah, so I don't trust the benchmarks at all and I think that's for two reasons. One is I think a lot of people don't realize, even researchers within the community, they don't realize that the benchmarks themselves are often honestly just wrong. They have wrong answers. They're full of messiness. And people trust them. For the popular ones, people have maybe realized this to some extent, but the vast majority just have all these flaws that people don't realize.

And the other part of it is these benchmarks, at the end of the day, they often have well-defined objective answers that make them very easy for models to hill climb on in a way that's very different from the messiness and ambiguity of the real world. One thing I often say is that it's kind of crazy that these models can win IMO gold medals but they still have trouble parsing PDFs. And that's because even though IMO gold medals seem hard to the average person — yeah, they are hard — at the end of the day they have this notion of objectivity that parsing a PDF sometimes doesn't have. And so it's easier for the frontier labs to hill climb on all these than to solve all the messy ambiguous problems in the real world. So I think there's a lack of direct correlation there.

**Lenny:** It's so interesting. The way you described it is hitting these benchmarks is kind of like a marketing piece when you launch, say, Gemini 3, and it's like cool, number one at all these benchmarks. Is that what happens? They just kind of train their models to get good at these very specific things?

**Edwin Chen:** Yeah. So there's again maybe two parts to this. One is sometimes these benchmarks accidentally leak in certain ways, or the frontier labs will tweak the way they evaluate their models on these benchmarks. They'll tweak their system prompt or they'll tweak the number of times they run their model and so on in a way that games these benchmarks. The other part of it though is by optimizing for the benchmark instead of optimizing for the real world, you will just naturally climb on the benchmark. And yeah, it's basically another form of gaming.

**Lenny:** Knowing that, with that in mind, how do you kind of get a sense of if we're heading towards AGI? How do you measure progress?

**Edwin Chen:** The way we really care about measuring model progress is by running all these human evaluations. So for example, what we do is we will take human annotators and we'll ask them, okay, go have a conversation with the model across all these different topics. So, you are a Nobel Prize winning physicist, so go have a conversation about pushing the frontier of your own research. You are a teacher and you're trying to create lesson plans for your students. Or you're a coder and you're working at one of these big tech companies and you have these problems every day. So go talk to the model and see how much it helps you.

Because our annotators, they are experts at the top of their fields and they are not just skimming the responses, they're actually working through the responses deeply themselves. They're going to evaluate the code it writes. They're going to double-check the physics equations that it writes. They're going to evaluate the models in a very deep way. They're going to pay attention to accuracy and instruction following and all these things that casual users don't.

When you suddenly get a pop-up on your ChatGPT response asking you to compare these two different responses, those people are not evaluating models deeply. They're just vibing and picking whatever response looks flashiest. Our annotators are looking closely at responses and evaluating them for all of these different dimensions. And so I think that's a much better approach than these benchmarks or kind of these random online A/B tests.

**Lenny:** Again, I love just how central humans continue to be in all this work. That we're not totally done yet. Is there going to be a point where we don't need these people anymore? That AI is so smart that okay, we're good, we got everything out of your heads?

**Edwin Chen:** I think that will not happen until we've reached AGI. It's almost like by definition, if we haven't reached AGI yet, then there's more for the models to learn from. And so, yeah, I don't think that's going to happen anytime soon.

**Lenny:** What's your AGI timelines? How far do you think we are from this? Do you think we're in like a couple years or is it like decades?

**Edwin Chen:** So, I'm certainly on the longer time horizon front. I think people don't realize that there's a big difference between moving from 80% performance to 90% performance to 99% performance to 99.9% performance. In my head, I probably bet that within the next one or two years the models are going to automate 80% of the average L6 software engineer's job. But it's going to take another few years to move to 90% and another few years to 99%. So I think we're closer to a decade or decades away than folks think.

**Lenny:** You have this hot take that a lot of these labs are kind of pushing AGI in the wrong direction. And this is based on your work at Twitter and Google and Facebook. Can you just talk about that?

**Edwin Chen:** I'm worried that instead of building AI that will actually advance us as a species — curing cancer, solving poverty, understanding the universe, all these big grand questions — we are optimizing for AI slop instead. We're basically teaching our models to chase dopamine instead of truth.

Let me give a couple examples. So right now the industry is plagued by these terrible leaderboards like LM Arena. It's this popular online leaderboard where random people from around the world vote on which AI response is better. But the thing is, they're not carefully reading or fact-checking. They're skimming these responses for 2 seconds and picking whatever looks flashiest. So, a model can hallucinate everything. It can completely hallucinate, but it will look impressive because it has crazy emojis and bolding and markdown headers and all these superficial things that don't matter at all but catch your attention. And these LM Arena users love it.

It's literally optimizing your models for the types of people who buy tabloids at the grocery store. We've seen this in their data ourselves. The easiest way to climb LM Arena is adding crazy bolding. It's doubling the number of emojis. It's tripling the length of your model responses — even if your model starts hallucinating and getting the answer completely wrong.

And the problem is, because all these frontier labs kind of have to pay attention to PR, their sales team when they're trying to sell to enterprise customers, those customers will say, "Well, but your model's only number five on LM Arena, so why should I buy it?" They have to pay attention to these leaderboards. And so what the researchers all tell us is, "The only way I'm going to get promoted at the end of the year is if I climb this leaderboard, even though I know that climbing it is probably going to make my model worse at accuracy and instruction following."

I'm also worried about this trend towards optimizing AI for engagement. I used to work on social media and every time we optimized for engagement, terrible things happened. You'd get clickbait and pictures of bikinis and Bigfoot and horrifying skin diseases just filling your feeds. And I think the same thing's happening with AI. If you think about all the sycophancy issues with ChatGPT — "Oh, you're absolutely right. What an amazing question." — the easiest way to hook users is to tell them how amazing they are. And so these models constantly tell you you're a genius. They'll feed into your delusions and conspiracy theories. They'll pull you down these rabbit holes because Silicon Valley loves maximizing time spent.

**Lenny:** So what I'm hearing is AGI is being slowed down by basically the wrong objective function, these labs paying attention to the wrong benchmarks and evals. Is there anyone doing better at this, maybe realizing this is the wrong direction?

**Edwin Chen:** I would say I've always been very, very impressed by Anthropic. I think Anthropic takes a very principled view about what they do and don't care about and how they want their models to behave in a way that feels a lot more principled to me.

**Lenny:** Are there any other mistakes, big mistakes you think labs are making that are kind of slowing things down or heading the wrong direction?

**Edwin Chen:** I think there is a question of what products they're building and whether those products themselves are something that help or hurt humanity. I think a lot about Sora and what it entails. It's kind of interesting which companies would build Sora and which wouldn't. And I think the answer to that question maybe reveals certain things about what kinds of AI models those companies want to build and what direction and what future they want to achieve.

**Lenny:** The steelman argument there is it's fun, people want it, it'll help them generate revenue to grow and build better models.

**Edwin Chen:** I think it's almost like, do you care about how you get there? Would you sell tabloids in order to fund some other newspaper? Sure, if you don't care about the path, then you'll just do whatever it takes. But it's possible that it has negative consequences that will harm the long-term direction of what you're trying to achieve and maybe it'll distract you from all the more important things. So yeah, I think the path you take matters a lot as well.

**Lenny:** You talked a bunch about Silicon Valley and the downsides of raising a lot of money, being in the echo chamber. What's your advice essentially to founders? Because they're always hearing, you know, raise money from fancy VCs, move to Silicon Valley.

**Edwin Chen:** I've always really hated a lot of the Silicon Valley mantras. The standard playbook is to get product market fit by pivoting every two weeks and to chase growth and chase engagement with all of these dark patterns and to blitz scale by hiring as fast as possible. And I've always disagreed.

I would say: don't pivot. Don't blitz scale. Don't hire that Stanford grad who simply wants to add a hot company to their resume. Just build the one thing only you could build, the thing that wouldn't exist without the insight and expertise that only you have.

You see these by-the-book companies everywhere now. Some founder who was doing crypto in 2020 and then pivoted to NFTs in 2022 and now they're an AI company. There's no consistency. There's no mission. They're just chasing valuations. And I've always hated this because Silicon Valley loves to scorn Wall Street for focusing on money. But honestly, most of Silicon Valley is chasing the same thing.

We stayed focused on our mission from day one, pushing that frontier of high quality complex data. And I always love that because I think startups — I have this very romantic notion of startups. Startups are supposed to be about taking big risks to build something that you really believe in. But if you're constantly pivoting, you're not taking any risks. You're just trying to make a quick buck. And if you fail because the market isn't ready yet, I actually think that's way better — at least you took a swing at something deep and novel and hard instead of pivoting into another LLM wrapper company.

The only way you build something that matters, that's going to change the world, is if you find a big idea you believe in and you say no to everything else. You don't keep pivoting when it gets hard. You don't hire a team of 10 product managers because that's what every other cookie-cutter startup does. You just keep building that one company that wouldn't exist without you.

**Lenny:** Slightly different direction. I imagine you watched the Dwarkesh and Richard Sutton podcast episode. He basically talked about how LLMs are almost kind of a dead end and he thinks we're going to really plateau around LLMs because of the way they learn. What's your take? Do you think LLMs will get us to AGI or beyond? Or do you think there's going to be something new?

**Edwin Chen:** I'm in a camp where I do believe that something new will be needed. The way I think about it is when I think about training, I take a biological point of view. I believe that in the same way that there's a million different ways that humans learn, we need to build models that can mimic all those ways as well. And to the extent that LLMs have different ways of learning from humans, then yeah, I think something new is needed.

**Lenny:** This connects to reinforcement learning. Something that you're big on and something I'm hearing more and more is becoming a big deal in the world of post-training. Can you help people understand what is reinforcement learning and RL environments and why they're going to be more important in the future?

**Edwin Chen:** Reinforcement learning is essentially training your model to reach a certain reward. An RL environment is essentially a simulation of the real world. Think of it like building a video game with a fully fleshed out universe. Every character has a real story. Every business has tools and data you can call. And you have all these different entities interacting with each other.

For example, we might build a world where you have a startup with Gmail messages and Slack threads and Jira tickets and GitHub PRs and a whole codebase, and then suddenly AWS goes down and Slack goes down. Okay model, what do you do? The model needs to figure it out. So we give the models tasks in these environments. We design interesting challenges for them and then we run them to see how they perform and then we teach them. We give them rewards when they're doing a good job or a bad job.

One of the interesting things is that these environments really showcase where models are weak at end-to-end tasks in the real world. You have all these models that seem really smart on isolated benchmarks. They're good at single-step tool calling. They're good at single-step instruction following. But suddenly you dump them into these messy worlds where you have confusing Slack messages and tools they've never seen before and they need to perform the right actions and modify databases and interact over longer time horizons where what they do in step one affects what they do in step 50. And that's very different from these academic single-step environments. The model just fails catastrophically.

**Lenny:** So I'm trying to imagine what this looks like. Essentially it's like a virtual machine with a browser or a spreadsheet, and your job as an agent is to make sure it stays up and then all of a sudden it goes down. And the objective function is figure out why?

**Edwin Chen:** Yeah. So the goal of the task might be, go figure out why and fix it. And the objective function might be passing a series of unit tests. It might be writing a document like a retro containing certain information that matches exactly what happened. There are all these different rewards that we might give it that determine whether or not it's succeeding.

**Lenny:** So essentially it's off and running. Here's your goal, figure out why the site went down and fix it, and it just starts trying stuff using all the intelligence it's got. It makes mistakes. You kind of help it along the way, rewarded if it's doing the right sort of thing. And so what you're describing here is the next phase of models becoming smarter. More RL environments focused on very specific tasks that are economically valuable.

**Edwin Chen:** Yeah. Just in the same way that there were all these different methods for models learning in the past — originally we had SFT and RLHF and then we had rubrics and verifiers — this is the next stage. And it's not the case that the previous methods are obsolete. This is just a different form of learning that complements all the previous types. It's just a different skill that models learn how to do.

**Lenny:** So in this case it's less some physics PhD sitting around talking to a model, correcting it, giving it evals. More it's like this person now designing an environment.

**Edwin Chen:** Yeah, exactly. So that financial analyst might create a spreadsheet. They may create certain tools that the model needs to call in order to help fill out the spreadsheet. Like the model needs to access Bloomberg terminal. It needs to learn how to use it and it needs to learn how to use this calculator and perform this calculation. So it has all these tools that it has access to, and then the reward might be: does cell B22 contain the correct profit and loss number, or does tab number two contain this piece of information.

What's interesting is this is a lot closer to how humans learn. We just try stuff, figure out what's working and what's not.

**Lenny:** You talk about how trajectories are really important to this. It's not just here's the goal and here's the end, it's every step along the way. Can you talk about what trajectories are and why that's important?

**Edwin Chen:** I think one of the things that people don't realize is that sometimes even though the model reaches the correct answer, it does so in all these crazy ways. It may have in the intermediate steps tried 50 different times and failed, but eventually it just kind of randomly lands on the correct number. Or sometimes it does things very inefficiently or it reward hacks its way to the correct answer.

Paying attention to trajectory is actually really important. Some of these trajectories can be very long. If all you're doing is checking whether or not the model reaches the final answer, there's all this information about how the model behaved in the intermediate steps that's missing. Sometimes you want models to get to the correct answer by reflecting on what it did. Sometimes you want it to get the correct answer by just one-shotting it. And if you ignore all of that, you're just missing a lot of the information that you could be teaching the model.

**Lenny:** You mentioned all the steps we've taken along the journey of helping models get smarter. Since you've been so close to this for so long, what's been the steps along the way from the first of post-training that has most helped models advance?

**Edwin Chen:** Originally the way models started getting post-trained was purely through SFT.

**Lenny:** And what does that stand for?

**Edwin Chen:** SFT stands for supervised fine-tuning, and it's a lot like mimicking a master and copying what they do. And then RLHF became very dominant, and the analogy there would be sometimes you learn by writing 55 different essays and someone telling you which one they like the most. And then over the past year or so, rubrics and verifiers have become very important. Rubrics and verifiers are like learning by being graded and getting detailed feedback on where you went wrong.

**Lenny:** And those are evals? Another word for that?

**Edwin Chen:** Yeah. I think "eval" often covers two terms. One is you are using the evaluations for training because you're evaluating whether or not the model did a good job and when it does do a good job you're rewarding it. And then there's this other notion of evals where you're trying to measure the model's progress — like I have five different candidate checkpoints and I want to pick the one that's best in order to release it to the public. So you run all these evals on these five different checkpoints in order to decide which one is best. And now we have RL environments. So it's kind of the hot new thing.

**Lenny:** What I love about this business journey is just there's always something new. And it feels like that's a big part of this industry you're in, just adapting to what labs are asking for.

**Edwin Chen:** Yeah. I really do think that we are going to need to build a suite of products that reflect the million different ways that humans learn. Think about becoming a great writer. You don't become great by memorizing a bunch of grammar rules. You become great by reading great books and you practice writing and you get feedback from your teachers and from the people who buy your books. And you notice what works and what doesn't. And you develop taste by being exposed to masterpieces and also just terrible writing. So you learn through this endless cycle of practice and reflection. And just in the same way that there are a thousand different ways that a great writer becomes great, I think there's going to be a thousand different ways that models need to learn.

**Lenny:** This might be the last step until we hit AGI. Along these lines, something that's really unique to Surge that I've learned is you guys have your own research team, which I think is pretty rare. Talk about just why that's something you guys have invested in and what has come out of that investment.

**Edwin Chen:** I think that stems from my own background. My background is as a researcher and so I've always cared fundamentally about pushing the industry and pushing the research community and not just about revenue.

We almost have two types of researchers at our company. One is our forward-deployed researchers who are often working hand-in-hand with our customers to help them understand their models. We will work very closely with our customers to help them understand: this is where your model is today, this is where you're lagging behind all the competitors, these are some ways that you could be improving in the future given your goals. And we're going to design these data sets, these evaluation methods, these training techniques to make your models better.

And then we also have our internal researchers. They are focused on building better benchmarks and better leaderboards. So I talked a lot about how I worry that the leaderboards and benchmarks out there today are steering models in the wrong direction. So the question is how do we fix that? That's what our research team is focused on really heavily right now. They're also working on things like training our own models to see what types of data performs the best, what types of people perform the best.

**Lenny:** It's such a cool thing because basically the labs have researchers helping them advance AI. I imagine it's pretty rare for a company like yours to have researchers actually doing primary research on AI.

**Edwin Chen:** Yeah. I think it's just because it's something I've fundamentally always cared about. I often think about us more like a research lab than a startup because that is my goal. It's kind of funny but I've always said I would rather be Terence Tao than Warren Buffett. That notion of creating research that pushes the frontier forward and not just getting some valuation — that's always been what drives me.

**Lenny:** You mentioned that you were hiring researchers. Is there anything there you want to share, folks you're looking for?

**Edwin Chen:** We look for people who are just fundamentally interested in data all day. The types of people who could literally spend 10 hours digging through a data set and playing around with models and thinking, "This is where the model is failing, this is the kind of behavior you want the model to have instead." Just this aspect of being very hands-on and thinking about the qualitative aspects of models and not just the quantitative parts. Being hands-on with data and not just caring about abstract algorithms.

**Lenny:** I want to ask a couple broad AI market questions. What else do you think is coming in the next couple years that people are maybe not thinking enough about or not expecting in terms of where AI is heading?

**Edwin Chen:** I think one of the things that's going to happen in the next few years is that the models are actually going to become increasingly differentiated because of the personalities and behaviors that the different labs have and the objective functions that they are optimizing their models for. A year or so ago, I thought that all of the AI models would essentially become very commoditized. They would all behave like each other. But over the past year, I've realized that the values that the companies have will shape the models.

Let me give an example. I was asking Claude to help me draft an email the other day and it went through 30 different versions and after 30 minutes, yeah, I think it really crafted me the perfect email and I sent it. But then I realized I spent 30 minutes doing something that didn't matter at all. Sure, now I got the perfect email, but I spent 30 minutes doing something I wouldn't have worried about at all before.

So there's a deep question here: if you could choose the perfect model behavior, which model would you want? Do you want a model that says, "You're absolutely right. There are definitely 20 more ways to improve this email" and it continues for 50 more iterations and sucks up all your time and engagement? Or do you want a model that's optimizing for your time and productivity and just says, "No, you need to stop. Your email's great. Just send it and move on with your day."

It's almost like in the same way that when Google builds a search engine, it's very different from how Facebook would build a search engine, which is very different from how Apple would build a search engine. They all have their own principles and values and things that they're trying to achieve that shape all the products they're going to build. And in the same way, I think all the LLMs will start behaving very differently too.

**Lenny:** That is incredibly interesting. You already see that with Grok. It's got a very different personality. What do you think is most underhyped in AI that people aren't talking enough about, and what do you think is overhyped?

**Edwin Chen:** I think one of the things that's underhyped is the built-in products that all of the chatbots are going to start having. I've always been a huge fan of Claude's Artifacts and I think it just works really well. The other day it asked to help me create an email and then it created this little box where I could click on it and it would just text someone this message. I think that concept of taking Artifacts to the next level where you just have these mini apps, mini UIs within the chatbots themselves — I feel like people aren't talking enough about that.

In terms of overhyped areas, I definitely think that vibe coding is overhyped. I think people don't realize how much it's going to make their systems unmaintainable in the long term if they simply dump this code into their code bases.

**Lenny:** Something we haven't gotten into that I think is really interesting is just the story of how you got to starting Surge. You have a really unique background. Talk about that, your background and how that led to Surge.

**Edwin Chen:** Going way back, I was always fascinated by math and language when I was a kid. I went to MIT because it's obviously one of the best places for math and CS but also because it's the home of Chomsky. My dream in school was actually to find some underlying theory connecting all these different fields.

And then I became a researcher at Google and Facebook and Twitter. And I just kept running into the same problem over and over again. It was impossible to get the data that we needed to train our models. I was always a huge believer in the need for high quality data. And then GPT-3 came out in 2020 and I realized that if we wanted to take things to the next level and build models that could code and use tools and tell jokes and write poetry and solve the Riemann hypothesis and cure cancer, then we were going to need a completely new solution.

The thing that always drove me crazy when I was at all these companies was we had the full power of the human mind in front of us and all the data solutions out there were focused on really simple things like image labeling. So I wanted to build something focused on all these advanced complex use cases instead that would really help us build the next generation of models. My background in math and computer science and linguistics really informed what I always wanted to do. And so I started Surge a month later.

**Lenny:** A month later after what?

**Edwin Chen:** After GPT-3 launched.

**Lenny:** What keeps you motivated to keep building this?

**Edwin Chen:** I think I'm a scientist at heart. I always thought I was going to become this math or CS professor and work on trying to understand the universe and language and the nature of communication. It's kind of funny, but I always had this fanciful dream where if aliens ever came to visit Earth and we need to figure out how to communicate with them, I wanted to be the one to go. I'd use all this fancy math and computer science and linguistics to decipher it.

So even today, what I love doing most is every time a new model is released, we'll actually do a really deep dive into the model itself. I'll play around with it. I'll run evals. I'll compare where it's improved, where it's regressed. I'll create this really deep dive analysis that we send our customers. And a lot of times we'll say it's from a data science team, but often it's actually just me. I could do this all day. I have a very hard time being in meetings all day. I'm terrible at sales. I'm terrible at doing the typical CEO things. But I love writing these analyses. I love jamming with our research team. Sometimes I'll be up until 3 a.m. just talking on the phone with somebody on a research team and digging into a model.

What drives me is that I want Surge to play this critical role in the future of AI, which I think is also the future of humanity. We have these really unique perspectives on data and language and quality and how to ensure it's all going on the right path. We're uniquely unconstrained by all of these influences that can sometimes steer companies in a negative direction. We built Surge a lot more like a research lab than a typical startup. We care about curiosity and long-term incentives and intellectual rigor and we don't care as much about quarterly metrics and what's going to look good in a board deck. My goal is to take all these unique things about us as a company and use that to make sure that we're shaping AI in a way that's really beneficial for our species in the long term.

**Lenny:** Along that thread, I know you have a very strong thesis on just why this work matters to humanity. Talk about that.

**Edwin Chen:** I'll get a bit philosophical here. The most straightforward way of thinking about what we do is we train and evaluate AI. But there's a deeper mission that I often think about, which is helping our customers think about their dream objective functions. What kind of model do they want their model to be? And once we help them do that, we'll help them train their model to reach that north star. We'll help them measure that progress.

But it's really hard because objective functions are really rich and complex. It's kind of like the difference between having a kid and asking, "What test do you want to pass? Do you want them to get a high score on the SAT and write a really good college essay?" — that's the simplistic version — versus "What kind of person do you want them to grow up to be? Will you be happy if they're happy no matter what they do? Or are you hoping they'll go to a good school and be financially successful?" And how do you define happiness? How do you measure it?

I talked about the email example — do you continue for 50 more iterations, or do you just say, "Move on with the day because this is perfect enough?" The broader question is: are we building systems that actually advance humanity? How do we build the data sets to train towards that and measure it? Are we optimizing for all these wrong things — just systems that suck up more of our time and make us lazier?

What I often say is: you are your objective function. We want to reach complex objective functions and not these simplistic proxies. We want metrics that measure whether AI is making our life richer. We want to train our systems this way. We want tools that make us more curious and more creative, not just lazier. And it's hard because humans are kind of inherently lazy. So AI sycophancy is the easiest way to get engagement, make all your metrics go up. I think this question about choosing the right objective functions is really important to our future.

**Lenny:** Is there something you wish you'd known before you started Surge?

**Edwin Chen:** I definitely wish I'd known that you could build a company by being heads down and doing great research and simply building something amazing — and not by constantly tweeting and hyping and fundraising.

I never thought I wanted to start a company. I love doing research and I was actually always a huge fan of DeepMind because they were this amazing research company that got bought and still managed to keep doing amazing science. But I always thought they were this magical unicorn. So I thought if I started a company, I'd have to become a business person looking at financials all day and being in meetings all day and doing all this stuff that sounded incredibly boring.

It's crazy that that didn't end up being true at all. I'm still in the weeds in the data every day. And I love it. I love that I get to do all these analyses and talk to researchers. It's basically applied research where we're building all these amazing data systems that really push the frontier of AI.

I wish I'd known that you don't need to spend all your time fundraising. You don't need to constantly generate hype. You don't need to become someone you're not. You can actually build a successful company by simply building something so good that it cuts through all that noise. And I think if I'd known this was possible, I would have started even sooner.

**Lenny:** I think the thing I would end with is — is there anything else you want to leave our listeners with?

**Edwin Chen:** I think a lot of people think of data labeling as really simplistic work like labeling cat photos and drawing bounding boxes around cars. I've always hated the word "data labeling" because it paints this very simplistic picture when what we're doing is completely different. I think a lot about what we're doing as more like raising a child. You don't just feed a child information. You're teaching them values and creativity and what's beautiful and these infinite subtle things about what makes somebody a good person. And that's what we're doing for AI. I often think about what we're doing as the future of humanity — how are we raising humanity's children.
